renderings.plainTitle: Why weighted selection is useful

steps.text: Fixed-size or one-token-at-a-time methods can cause major problems when inputs vary in size and information content, such as text.

citation.quotedText: Variable size is addressed by sequentially processing one token at a time, or by specially designed convolution kernels . This approach can lead to significant problems when the input is truly of varying size with varying information content, such as in in the transformation of text .

steps.text: For long sequences, a network can have trouble keeping track of everything it has seen or generated.

citation.quotedText: In particular, for long sequences it becomes quite difficult to keep track of everything that has already been generated or even viewed by the network.

steps.text: Attention pooling lets a neural network select relevant items from a set and build a weighted sum from them.

citation.quotedText: By design, the attention mechanism provides a differentiable means of control by which a neural network can select elements from a set and to construct an associated weighted sum over representations.

steps.text: It can handle databases of any size without changing how the pooling operation works.

citation.quotedText: Just as convenient is the fact that attention can operate on arbitrarily large databases without the need to change the way the attention pooling operation is performed.

renderings.plainTitle: How attention pooling combines values

steps.text: It derives a weight for each value from how compatible the query is with that value’s key.

citation.quotedText: weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query $\mathbf{q}$ and keys $\mathbf{k}_\mathit{i}$.

steps.text: It multiplies each value by its weight and adds the results, producing a linear combination of the values.

citation.quotedText: Then we can define the attention over $\mathcal{D}$ as $$\textrm{Attention}(\mathbf{q}, \mathcal{D}) \stackrel{\textrm{def}}{=} \sum_{i=1}^m \alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) \mathbf{v}_i,$$

steps.text: In the most common deep-learning setting, the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1.

citation.quotedText: The weights $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i)$ form a convex combination, i.e., $\sum_i \alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) = 1$ and $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) \geq 0$ for all $i$. This is the most common setting in deep learning.

renderings.plainTitle: Why combine matching values into one result?

steps.text: The dot product lets you express a weighted sum of a set of values as one operation.

citation.quotedText: given some set of values, denoted by a vector $\mathbf{x}  \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and a set of weights, denoted by $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the weighted sum of the values in $\mathbf{x}$ according to the weights $\mathbf{w}$ could be expressed as the dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{w}$.

steps.text: When the weights are nonnegative and add up to 1, it also expresses a weighted average.

citation.quotedText: When the weights are nonnegative and sum to $1$, i.e., $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} {w_i} = 1\right)$, the dot product expresses a weighted average.

steps.text: It also provides the basic calculation used in a matrix–vector product: each output entry is a dot product between one matrix row and the vector.

citation.quotedText: The matrix--vector product $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ is simply a column vector of length $m$, whose $i^\textrm{th}$ element is the dot product $\mathbf{a}^\top_i \mathbf{x}$

renderings.plainTitle: How two vectors are combined into one number

steps.text: Multiply the elements that occupy the same position in the two vectors.

citation.quotedText: we can calculate the dot product of two vectors by performing an elementwise multiplication followed by a sum:

steps.text: Add those products together.

citation.quotedText: their dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y}$ (also known as inner product, $\langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y}  \rangle$) is a sum over the products of the elements at the same position: $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{y} = \sum_{i=1}^{d} x_i y_i$.

renderings.plainTitle: Why multiply a matrix by a vector?

steps.text: It provides a way to transform a vector into another vector, which may have a different length.

citation.quotedText: We can think of multiplication with a matrix $\mathbf{A}\in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}$ as a transformation that projects vectors from $\mathbb{R}^{n}$ to $\mathbb{R}^{m}$.

steps.text: These transformations can represent actions such as rotating a vector.

citation.quotedText: For example, we can represent rotations as multiplications by certain square matrices.

steps.text: They also perform the key calculation that turns one neural-network layer’s outputs into the next layer’s outputs.

citation.quotedText: Matrix--vector products also describe the key calculation involved in computing the outputs of each layer in a neural network given the outputs from the previous layer.

renderings.plainTitle: How a matrix and vector are multiplied

steps.text: Take each row of the matrix and calculate its dot product with the input vector.

citation.quotedText: whose $i^\textrm{th}$ element is the dot product $\mathbf{a}^\top_i \mathbf{x}$

steps.text: Place each result into the matching position of a column vector. The finished vector has one entry for each of the matrix’s $m$ rows.

citation.quotedText: The matrix--vector product $\mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}$ is simply a column vector of length $m$

renderings.plainTitle: Why the model needs clues about word order

steps.text: Self-attention favors parallel work instead of processing tokens one at a time.

citation.quotedText: Unlike RNNs, which recurrently process tokens of a sequence one-by-one, self-attention ditches sequential operations in favor of parallel computation.

steps.text: The problem is that self-attention alone does not keep track of the sequence’s order.

citation.quotedText: Note that self-attention by itself does not preserve the order of the sequence.

steps.text: When order matters, the dominant approach gives the model extra order information tied to each token.

citation.quotedText: The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

renderings.plainTitle: How token order is added

steps.text: Each token receives an additional input that carries information about its position in the sequence.

citation.quotedText: The dominant approach for preserving information about the order of tokens is to represent this to the model as an additional input associated with each token.

steps.text: The position inputs may be learned during training or fixed in advance.

citation.quotedText: These inputs are called positional encodings, and they can either be learned or fixed a priori.

steps.text: In the fixed scheme described here, sine and cosine functions produce a positional encoding matrix with one row per sequence position and columns for different encoding dimensions.

citation.quotedText: In the positional embedding matrix $\mathbf{P}$, rows correspond to positions within a sequence and columns represent different positional encoding dimensions.

steps.text: The positional encoding matrix is added to the matrix containing the token inputs.

citation.quotedText: The positional encoding outputs $\mathbf{X} + \mathbf{P}$ using a positional embedding matrix $\mathbf{P} \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ of the same shape

renderings.plainTitle: Why use queries, keys, and values?

steps.text: Networks that process one token at a time can struggle when the amount and content of input vary, especially because long sequences make past information difficult to track.

citation.quotedText: This approach can lead to significant problems when the input is truly of varying size with varying information content, such as in in the transformation of text . In particular, for long sequences it becomes quite difficult to keep track of everything that has already been generated or even viewed by the network.

steps.text: A query can work with key–value pairs no matter how large the collection is.

citation.quotedText: We can design queries $q$ that operate on ($k$,$v$) pairs in such a manner as to be valid regardless of the database size.

steps.text: The collection does not need to be compressed or simplified for queries to work effectively.

citation.quotedText: There is no need to compress or simplify the database to make the operations effective.

steps.text: The query can stay concise even when searching a large collection, so a network layer does not need too many parameters to learn.

citation.quotedText: What is quite remarkable is that the actual "code" for executing on the set of keys and values, namely the query, can be quite concise, even though the space to operate on is significant. This is a desirable property for a network layer as it does not require too many parameters to learn.

renderings.plainTitle: How a Query Uses Keys and Values

steps.text: The data is arranged as a database of key–value pairs, and a query is provided.

citation.quotedText: denote by $\mathcal{D} \stackrel{\textrm{def}}{=} \{(\mathbf{k}_1, \mathbf{v}_1), \ldots (\mathbf{k}_m, \mathbf{v}_m)\}$ a database of $m$ tuples of keys and values. Moreover, denote by $\mathbf{q}$ a query.

steps.text: A weight is derived from how compatible the query is with each key.

citation.quotedText: weights are derived according to the compatibility between a query $\mathbf{q}$ and keys $\mathbf{k}_\mathit{i}$.

steps.text: Attention pooling multiplies each value by its weight and adds the results together.

citation.quotedText: The attention mechanism computes a linear combination over values $\mathbf{v}_\mathit{i}$ via attention pooling

steps.text: In the most common deep-learning setting, the weights are at least zero and add up to 1.

citation.quotedText: The weights $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i)$ form a convex combination, i.e., $\sum_i \alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) = 1$ and $\alpha(\mathbf{q}, \mathbf{k}_i) \geq 0$ for all $i$. This is the most common setting in deep learning.

renderings.plainTitle: Why let every token connect directly?

steps.text: CNNs and RNNs often encoded sequences before attention-based models.

citation.quotedText: In deep learning, we often use CNNs or RNNs to encode sequences.

steps.text: Self-attention gives each token a direct connection to every other token, which allows parallel computation and makes the longest path between tokens as short as possible.

citation.quotedText: As we can see from , each token is directly connected to any other token via self-attention. Therefore, computation can be parallel with $\mathcal{O}(1)$ sequential operations and the maximum path length is also $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

steps.text: This shorter path makes it easier to learn relationships between distant parts of a sequence.

citation.quotedText: a shorter path between any combination of sequence positions makes it easier to learn long-range dependencies within the sequence

steps.text: The tradeoff is that self-attention becomes prohibitively slow for very long sequences because its work grows with the square of sequence length.

citation.quotedText: However, the quadratic computational complexity with respect to the sequence length makes self-attention prohibitively slow for very long sequences.

renderings.plainTitle: How each token uses the whole sequence

steps.text: The sequence enters the attention mechanism, where each token has its own query, keys, and values.

citation.quotedText: imagine feeding a sequence of tokens into an attention mechanism such that at every step, each token has its own query, keys, and values.

steps.text: For a token’s next representation, its query can attend to any other token by matching against that token’s keys.

citation.quotedText: Here, when computing the value of a token's representation at the next layer, the token can attend (via its query vector) to any other's token (matching based on their key vectors).

steps.text: For each token, the full set of query-key compatibility scores determines a weighted sum over the other tokens, producing its representation.

citation.quotedText: Using the full set of query-key compatibility scores, we can compute, for each token, a representation by building the appropriate weighted sum over the other tokens.

steps.text: The output is a sequence with the same number of tokens as the input.

citation.quotedText: Given a sequence of input tokens $\mathbf{x}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{x}_n$ where any $\mathbf{x}_i \in \mathbb{R}^d$ ($1 \leq i \leq n$), its self-attention outputs a sequence of the same length $\mathbf{y}_1, \ldots, \mathbf{y}_n$

renderings.plainTitle: Why model scores need to become probabilities

steps.text: Using the model’s raw outputs directly is unsatisfactory because they may not add up to 1, as probabilities should.

citation.quotedText: There is no guarantee that the outputs $o_i$ sum up to $1$ in the way we expect probabilities to behave.

steps.text: Raw outputs may also be negative or greater than 1, making them unsuitable as probabilities.

citation.quotedText: There is no guarantee that the outputs $o_i$ are even nonnegative, even if their outputs sum up to $1$, or that they do not exceed $1$.

steps.text: A previous approach added normally distributed noise to the outputs, but it did not work as well or produce as nice an optimization problem as softmax.

citation.quotedText: While appealing, it does not work quite as well nor lead to a particularly nice optimization problem, when compared to the softmax.

steps.text: Softmax exists to turn ordinary model outputs into valid probability distributions over separate categories.

citation.quotedText: As a side effect, we encountered the softmax, a convenient activation function that transforms outputs of an ordinary neural network layer into valid discrete probability distributions.

renderings.plainTitle: How softmax changes a set of outputs

steps.text: Raise the mathematical constant e to the power of each output.

citation.quotedText: Another way to accomplish this goal (and to ensure nonnegativity) is to use an exponential function $P(y = i) \propto \exp o_i$.

steps.text: Divide each resulting value by the sum of all those values, making them add up to 1.

citation.quotedText: We can then transform these values so that they add up to $1$ by dividing each by their sum.

renderings.plainTitle: Pick the most likely class with less work

steps.text: Softmax keeps class scores in the same order, so the largest score still points to the most likely class.

citation.quotedText: Note that the largest coordinate of $\mathbf{o}$ corresponds to the most likely class according to $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

steps.text: Because that order does not change, you can identify the highest-probability class without calculating softmax.

citation.quotedText: Moreover, because the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments, we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability.

renderings.plainTitle: How the Largest Score Stays on Top

steps.text: Softmax preserves the order of the input scores.

citation.quotedText: the softmax operation preserves the ordering among its arguments

steps.text: As a result, the largest input score points to the class with the highest probability.

citation.quotedText: Note that the largest coordinate of $\mathbf{o}$ corresponds to the most likely class according to $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$.

steps.text: You can therefore find the highest-probability class without calculating softmax.

citation.quotedText: we do not need to compute the softmax to determine which class has been assigned the highest probability.

renderings.plainTitle: Why We Need One Number for Size and Distance

steps.text: A vector norm gives a way to capture how large a vector or matrix is.

citation.quotedText: Norms capture various notions of the magnitude of a vector (or matrix)

steps.text: Different norms are useful because there is more than one meaningful way to measure size.

citation.quotedText: Many functions are valid norms and different norms encode different notions of size.

steps.text: Norms can measure how far apart two vectors are by measuring the size of their difference.

citation.quotedText: are commonly applied to the difference of two vectors to measure their distance apart.

steps.text: This matters in deep learning because distances used as learning goals are often expressed as norms.

citation.quotedText: These distances, which constitute the objectives of deep learning algorithms, are often expressed as norms.

renderings.plainTitle: How a Vector’s Size Is Calculated

steps.text: For the ℓ₂ norm, square each element, add the squares, and take the square root.

citation.quotedText: The Euclidean norm that we all learned in elementary school geometry when calculating the hypotenuse of a right triangle is the square root of the sum of squares of a vector's elements.

steps.text: For the ℓ₁ norm, take the absolute value of each element and add those values.

citation.quotedText: By definition, the $\ell_1$ norm sums the absolute values of a vector's elements:

renderings.plainTitle: Why group related numbers together?

steps.text: Vectors let one real-world example carry several meaningful measurements together, such as a loan applicant’s income, employment length, and previous defaults.

citation.quotedText: For example, if we were training a model to predict the risk of a loan defaulting, we might associate each applicant with a vector whose components correspond to quantities like their income, length of employment, or number of previous defaults.

steps.text: They also make weighted sums and weighted averages possible through the dot product.

citation.quotedText: For example, given some set of values, denoted by a vector $\mathbf{x}  \in \mathbb{R}^n$, and a set of weights, denoted by $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, the weighted sum of the values in $\mathbf{x}$ according to the weights $\mathbf{w}$ could be expressed as the dot product $\mathbf{x}^\top \mathbf{w}$. When the weights are nonnegative and sum to $1$, i.e., $\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} {w_i} = 1\right)$, the dot product expresses a weighted average.

steps.text: Vectors can be transformed with a matrix, a calculation used to produce each neural-network layer’s outputs from the previous layer.

citation.quotedText: Matrix--vector products also describe the key calculation involved in computing the outputs of each layer in a neural network given the outputs from the previous layer.

renderings.plainTitle: How a vector is stored and accessed

steps.text: A vector holds a fixed number of scalar values in an array.

citation.quotedText: For current purposes, you can think of a vector as a fixed-length array of scalars.

steps.text: Each scalar value is treated as an element of the vector.

citation.quotedText: As with their code counterparts, we call these scalars the elements of the vector (synonyms include entries and components).

steps.text: Code stores a vector as a tensor with one axis.

citation.quotedText: Vectors are implemented as $1^{\textrm{st}}$-order tensors.

steps.text: Code accesses individual elements by their index; in Python, indexing starts at 0.

citation.quotedText: Caution: in Python, as in most programming languages, vector indices start at $0$, also known as zero-based indexing, whereas in linear algebra subscripts begin at $1$ (one-based indexing).
